  1. BCB744: Biostatistics
  2. 10. Choosing the Right Test

10. Choosing the Right Test

A Practical Decision Guide for Core Inferential Questions

Author

A. J. Smit

Published

2026/03/20

NoteIn This Chapter
  • How to connect the biological question to the statistical method
  • Choosing among the core methods already introduced
  • When to consider non-parametric alternatives
  • How this chapter links the inferential material to the modelling chapters
ImportantTasks to Complete in This Chapter
  • None

1 Introduction

Many students first encounter statistics as a menu of named tests. That is not the best way to choose a method. In practice, test selection begins with the biological question, the structure of the data, and the design of the study.

By the time you reach this chapter, you have already met the main inferential tools used in the first part of the course:

  • one-sample, two-sample, and paired t-tests;
  • ANOVA and its common extensions;
  • correlation and association;
  • proportion tests;
  • their most common non-parametric alternatives.

This chapter draws those threads together. Its purpose is to help you choose among the tests you already know.

2 Key Concepts

The practical rule is to choose a method by question, data structure, and design together.

  • Test selection starts with the question, not the software function.
  • Variable type matters because some methods are only appropriate for continuous data, others for counts, categories, or binary outcomes.
  • Design matters because independence, pairing, and repeated measurement change the appropriate analysis.
  • Assumptions matter because they determine whether a parametric method is reasonable or whether an alternative is needed.
  • Methods connect because the logic of one-sample tests, group comparisons, association, and modelling forms a sequence rather than a collection of unrelated techniques.

3 The Decision Sequence

Before naming a test, work through the following questions in order:

  1. What is the biological question?
  2. What is the response variable, and what kind of data is it?
  3. How many groups, variables, or predictors are involved?
  4. Are the observations independent, paired, or repeated?
  5. Are the assumptions of the preferred method plausible?

If you can answer those five questions clearly, the correct family of methods is usually obvious.

4 Choosing by Question Type

4.1 Is the question about one mean or one proportion?

If you have a single sample and want to compare it against a known or hypothesised value:

  • use a one-sample t-test for a continuous response when the assumptions are plausible;
  • use a one-sample Wilcoxon signed-rank test when the one-sample t-test assumptions fail badly;
  • use a one-sample proportion test when the response is binary and the question concerns a probability of success.

This is the logic developed in 07-t_tests.qmd.

4.2 Is the question about two groups?

If you want to compare two groups:

  • use an independent two-sample t-test when the groups are independent and the response is continuous;
  • use a paired t-test when the same individuals are measured twice or when observations are deliberately matched;
  • use a Wilcoxon rank-sum test as a non-parametric alternative for two independent groups;
  • use a Wilcoxon signed-rank test for non-parametric paired data;
  • use a two-sample proportion test when the response is binary and the question concerns two proportions.

Again, this logic comes directly out of 07-t_tests.qmd.

4.3 Is the question about three or more groups?

If the response is continuous and the predictor is a categorical factor with three or more levels:

  • use a one-way ANOVA when the assumptions are plausible;
  • use a factorial ANOVA when more than one categorical factor is involved;
  • use a Kruskal-Wallis test when a one-way ANOVA is not appropriate and a rank-based alternative is acceptable.

This is the territory of 08-anova.qmd.

4.4 Is the question about association between two variables?

If the question is whether two variables vary together:

  • use Pearson correlation for approximately linear association among continuous variables;
  • use Spearman or Kendall correlation when rank-based association is more appropriate;
  • move to regression if the real question is one of response and predictor rather than simple association.

This is the logic developed in 09-correlation-and-association.qmd.

5 Choosing by Data Structure

Another way to choose a method is to start with the data structure.

5.1 Continuous response with a categorical predictor

This is the classical comparison-of-means setting:

  • one group against a reference value → one-sample t-test;
  • two groups → two-sample t-test;
  • paired measurements → paired t-test;
  • three or more groups → ANOVA.

5.2 Continuous response with a continuous explanatory variable

This is where the distinction between association and modelling becomes important:

  • use correlation when the goal is to quantify association;
  • use regression when the goal is to model a response.

5.3 Binary response

When the response is a success/failure outcome summarised as a proportion:

  • use proportion tests for one-sample or two-sample proportion questions;
  • move later to generalised linear models when the structure becomes more complicated.

6 Assumptions and Alternatives

Parametric methods are often preferred because they use more information from the data and are usually more powerful when their assumptions are reasonable. But this does not mean that you force a parametric method onto unsuitable data.

The practical sequence is:

  1. choose the method that matches the question and design;
  2. check whether its assumptions are plausible;
  3. if necessary, consider a transformation, a rank-based alternative, or a more suitable model.

For the material covered so far, the most important alternatives are:

  • one-sample or paired t-test → wilcox.test();
  • two-sample t-test → wilcox.test();
  • one-way ANOVA → kruskal.test();
  • Pearson correlation → Spearman or Kendall correlation.

Assumption checking itself is discussed in 06-assumptions-and-transformations.qmd.

7 Common Mistakes in Test Selection

These are the most common reasons students choose the wrong method:

  • choosing a test because the function name is familiar rather than because the question fits;
  • ignoring whether the observations are paired or independent;
  • using correlation when the real aim is regression;
  • using many pairwise t-tests instead of ANOVA;
  • switching to a non-parametric test without first understanding which assumption has failed;
  • treating a binary response as if it were continuous.

8 Where This Leads Next

This chapter closes the first inferential block of the course. The next chapters move into modelling:

  • 11-simple-linear-regression.qmd
  • 12-polynomial-regression.qmd
  • 13-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd

Later in the sequence, those core modelling chapters extend into specialised models, then into:

  • 23-prediction-and-explanation.qmd
  • 24-regularisation.qmd
  • 25-reproducible-workflow.qmd

That transition matters. Once your question becomes one of explicit response modelling rather than group comparison or simple association, you are leaving the world of named introductory tests and entering the world of models.

9 Summary

Choose a method in this order:

  1. Start with the biological question.
  2. Identify the response type and explanatory structure.
  3. Decide whether observations are independent, paired, or repeated.
  4. Match the problem to the correct method family.
  5. Check assumptions and use alternatives only for a clear reason.

If you follow this sequence, you will usually arrive at the right test without having to memorise statistics as a disconnected list.

---

```{r code-brewing-opts, echo=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  comment = "R>",
  warning = FALSE,
  message = FALSE,
  fig.asp = NULL,
  fig.align = "center",
  fig.retina = 2,
  dpi = 300
)

ggplot2::theme_set(
  ggplot2::theme_grey(base_size = 8)
)
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
## In This Chapter

-   How to connect the biological question to the statistical method
-   Choosing among the core methods already introduced
-   When to consider non-parametric alternatives
-   How this chapter links the inferential material to the modelling chapters
:::

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
## Tasks to Complete in This Chapter

-   None
:::

# Introduction

Many students first encounter statistics as a menu of named tests. That is not the best way to choose a method. In practice, test selection begins with the biological question, the structure of the data, and the design of the study.

By the time you reach this chapter, you have already met the main inferential tools used in the first part of the course:

-   one-sample, two-sample, and paired *t*-tests;
-   ANOVA and its common extensions;
-   correlation and association;
-   proportion tests;
-   their most common non-parametric alternatives.

This chapter draws those threads together. Its purpose is to help you choose among the tests you already know.

# Key Concepts

The practical rule is to choose a method by question, data structure, and design together.

-   **Test selection starts with the question**, not the software function.
-   **Variable type matters** because some methods are only appropriate for continuous data, others for counts, categories, or binary outcomes.
-   **Design matters** because independence, pairing, and repeated measurement change the appropriate analysis.
-   **Assumptions matter** because they determine whether a parametric method is reasonable or whether an alternative is needed.
-   **Methods connect** because the logic of one-sample tests, group comparisons, association, and modelling forms a sequence rather than a collection of unrelated techniques.

# The Decision Sequence

Before naming a test, work through the following questions in order:

1. What is the biological question?
2. What is the response variable, and what kind of data is it?
3. How many groups, variables, or predictors are involved?
4. Are the observations independent, paired, or repeated?
5. Are the assumptions of the preferred method plausible?

If you can answer those five questions clearly, the correct family of methods is usually obvious.

# Choosing by Question Type

## Is the question about one mean or one proportion?

If you have a single sample and want to compare it against a known or hypothesised value:

-   use a **one-sample *t*-test** for a continuous response when the assumptions are plausible;
-   use a **one-sample Wilcoxon signed-rank test** when the one-sample *t*-test assumptions fail badly;
-   use a **one-sample proportion test** when the response is binary and the question concerns a probability of success.

This is the logic developed in [07-t_tests.qmd](07-t_tests.qmd).

## Is the question about two groups?

If you want to compare two groups:

-   use an **independent two-sample *t*-test** when the groups are independent and the response is continuous;
-   use a **paired *t*-test** when the same individuals are measured twice or when observations are deliberately matched;
-   use a **Wilcoxon rank-sum test** as a non-parametric alternative for two independent groups;
-   use a **Wilcoxon signed-rank test** for non-parametric paired data;
-   use a **two-sample proportion test** when the response is binary and the question concerns two proportions.

Again, this logic comes directly out of [07-t_tests.qmd](07-t_tests.qmd).

## Is the question about three or more groups?

If the response is continuous and the predictor is a categorical factor with three or more levels:

-   use a **one-way ANOVA** when the assumptions are plausible;
-   use a **factorial ANOVA** when more than one categorical factor is involved;
-   use a **Kruskal-Wallis test** when a one-way ANOVA is not appropriate and a rank-based alternative is acceptable.

This is the territory of [08-anova.qmd](08-anova.qmd).

## Is the question about association between two variables?

If the question is whether two variables vary together:

-   use **Pearson correlation** for approximately linear association among continuous variables;
-   use **Spearman** or **Kendall** correlation when rank-based association is more appropriate;
-   move to **regression** if the real question is one of response and predictor rather than simple association.

This is the logic developed in [09-correlation-and-association.qmd](09-correlation-and-association.qmd).

# Choosing by Data Structure

Another way to choose a method is to start with the data structure.

## Continuous response with a categorical predictor

This is the classical comparison-of-means setting:

-   one group against a reference value → one-sample *t*-test;
-   two groups → two-sample *t*-test;
-   paired measurements → paired *t*-test;
-   three or more groups → ANOVA.

## Continuous response with a continuous explanatory variable

This is where the distinction between association and modelling becomes important:

-   use **correlation** when the goal is to quantify association;
-   use **regression** when the goal is to model a response.

## Binary response

When the response is a success/failure outcome summarised as a proportion:

-   use **proportion tests** for one-sample or two-sample proportion questions;
-   move later to **generalised linear models** when the structure becomes more complicated.

# Assumptions and Alternatives

Parametric methods are often preferred because they use more information from the data and are usually more powerful when their assumptions are reasonable. But this does not mean that you force a parametric method onto unsuitable data.

The practical sequence is:

1. choose the method that matches the question and design;
2. check whether its assumptions are plausible;
3. if necessary, consider a transformation, a rank-based alternative, or a more suitable model.

For the material covered so far, the most important alternatives are:

-   one-sample or paired *t*-test → `wilcox.test()`;
-   two-sample *t*-test → `wilcox.test()`;
-   one-way ANOVA → `kruskal.test()`;
-   Pearson correlation → Spearman or Kendall correlation.

Assumption checking itself is discussed in [06-assumptions-and-transformations.qmd](06-assumptions-and-transformations.qmd).

# Common Mistakes in Test Selection

These are the most common reasons students choose the wrong method:

-   choosing a test because the function name is familiar rather than because the question fits;
-   ignoring whether the observations are paired or independent;
-   using correlation when the real aim is regression;
-   using many pairwise *t*-tests instead of ANOVA;
-   switching to a non-parametric test without first understanding which assumption has failed;
-   treating a binary response as if it were continuous.

# Where This Leads Next

This chapter closes the first inferential block of the course. The next chapters move into modelling:

-   [11-simple-linear-regression.qmd](11-simple-linear-regression.qmd)
-   [12-polynomial-regression.qmd](12-polynomial-regression.qmd)
-   [13-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd](13-multiple-regression-and-model-specification.qmd)

Later in the sequence, those core modelling chapters extend into specialised models, then into:

-   [23-prediction-and-explanation.qmd](23-prediction-and-explanation.qmd)
-   [24-regularisation.qmd](24-regularisation.qmd)
-   [25-reproducible-workflow.qmd](25-reproducible-workflow.qmd)

That transition matters. Once your question becomes one of explicit response modelling rather than group comparison or simple association, you are leaving the world of named introductory tests and entering the world of models.

# Summary

Choose a method in this order:

1. Start with the biological question.
2. Identify the response type and explanatory structure.
3. Decide whether observations are independent, paired, or repeated.
4. Match the problem to the correct method family.
5. Check assumptions and use alternatives only for a clear reason.

If you follow this sequence, you will usually arrive at the right test without having to memorise statistics as a disconnected list.
